ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers date fruit broadly; dried form is the closest defensible match within the FoodOn hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 102263004 refers to dates as a food substance; dried form is the applicable preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of dried dates is approximately 30 g (3–4 whole dates), providing around 87 kcal and approximately 19.8 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Dates, dried — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04030019 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dried dates have a published GI of 42 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). GI range for dried dates is reported as 42–62 depending on variety; GI 42 (Khalas variety) is used as the conservative lower-bound reference. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (42 × 20.16 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 8.5, rounded to 8.4 using HPB carbohydrate values (67.2 g/100g × 0.30 = 20.16 g available carbohydrate; GL = 42 × 20.16 / 100 = 8.47 ≈ 8.4).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (65.9 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, partially offsetting the benefit of high potassium (730 mg/100g); sodium is low at 14 mg/100g.
hypertension.guidance: Limit dried dates to no more than 1–2 pieces (approximately 15–20 g) per occasion; while potassium content is beneficial for blood pressure regulation, the extreme sugar load promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and should be restricted in hypertensive individuals following a low-sugar dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 65.9 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; despite a low GI of 42 and meaningful dietary fibre at 9.7 g/100g, the concentrated sugar and high glycaemic load at typical serving sizes cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried dates should be avoided as a routine snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, restrict to one date (approximately 8 g) paired with a protein or fat source to attenuate glycaemic response, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free and very low in fat (0.2 g/100g) with high potassium (730 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (9.7 g/100g), which are cardioprotective; however, very high sugar content (65.9 g/100g) raises triglyceride risk with regular or large-portion consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried dates may be consumed in small portions (1–2 dates, approximately 15–20 g) as part of a heart-healthy diet given their fibre and potassium content, but regular large-portion intake should be avoided due to the high sugar load that can elevate serum triglycerides and contribute to metabolic cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar content of 65.9 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine production; polyphenol and fibre content provide partial anti-inflammatory offset.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Limit to 1–2 dates maximum per day if consumed; pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as walnuts or fatty fish, and consider fresh dates as a lower-sugar alternative in very small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried dates are dehydrated stone fruits derived from the date palm (Phoenix dactylifera), characterised by a dark brown colour, soft and chewy texture, and an intensely sweet flavour profile with caramel and honey-like notes. The dehydration process concentrates the natural sugars — predominantly fructose and glucose — resulting in a sugar content of approximately 65.9 g per 100 g, making dried dates one of the most sugar-dense whole foods available. Despite this, they retain meaningful dietary fibre (9.7 g/100g), potassium (730 mg/100g), calcium (47 mg/100g), and iron (2.6 mg/100g), contributing to their nutritional complexity. Dried dates are widely consumed across the Middle East, North Africa, and South Asia as a traditional snack, a natural sweetener in cooking and baking, and a culturally significant food during Ramadan and other religious observances. In Singapore and Southeast Asia, they are commonly available as packaged snacks and are used in health-food preparations. Clinically, dried dates carry a low glycaemic index (GI 42) due to their fibre and fructose composition, but their high glycaemic load at typical serving sizes makes them unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood sugar. Their high potassium content is beneficial for blood pressure, but the extreme sugar load limits their suitability for hypertensive individuals on low-sugar diets. They are cholesterol-free, very low in fat, and suitable for vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free dietary patterns.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95 in the input record. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for dried dates are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for F04030019 as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; dates are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'wholegrain' which is not applicable to dates and has been excluded from classification. GI of 42 represents the Khalas variety lower bound; GI range across varieties is 42–62 (low to medium). Glycaemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (42 × 20.16) / 100 = 8.47, rounded to 8.4.
